Title: Sealing Plant Surfaces: Cuticular Wax Formation by Epidermal Cells

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for wax export from epidermal cells; cer5 mutants have reduced surface wax load and intracellular wax accumulation, particularly reduced alkanes in stem wax
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant analysis; functional fluorescent protein fusion localization
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CER5 is a plasma membrane ABC transporter required for normal surface wax deposition. 2. cer5 mutants accumulate wax intracellularly and have lower stem wax loads. 3. CER5 likely acts together with WBC11 in the same pathway or complex, potentially as a heterodimer.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct transport of wax molecules was inferred but not directly demonstrated in this review.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for wax export; wbc11 mutants show reduced stem wax load with intracellular wax accumulation and additionally display cutin-related defects
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant analysis; functional fluorescent protein fusion localization; double-mutant analysis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. WBC11 is a plasma membrane transporter required for normal wax export. 2. Mutants show reduced surface wax and intracellular wax accumulation. 3. WBC11 may form a heterodimer with CER5 for wax export and may also have an additional role in cutin export.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: wbc11 mutants exhibit organ fusions and decreased cutin levels
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review proposes but does not prove heterodimerization with CER5.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Protein of unknown precise biochemical function
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: cer1 mutants have drastically reduced stem wax with reduced all stem wax components and C30 aldehyde accumulation, implicating CER1 in the alkane-forming pathway
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CER1 is required for normal alkane-branch wax formation. 2. cer1 mutants accumulate aldehydes and are depleted in alkanes and related products. 3. The exact biochemical function remained unresolved in this review, although CER1 was considered a likely pathway enzyme.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although likely an enzyme in alkane formation, the review explicitly states the function was not characterized; therefore classified here as Common_Gene.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Protein of unknown precise biochemical function
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: cer2 mutants have major reductions in alkanes, secondary alcohols and ketones with accumulation of C26/C28 acyl groups, primary alcohols and wax esters
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant analysis; expression analysis; gene cloning
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: transcriptionally regulated during development; not induced by light, heat, cold, wound, or osmotic stress
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CER2 is important for production of longer-chain wax components in the alkane branch. 2. Mutation causes depletion of alkane-derived products and accumulation of shorter-chain acyl and alcohol/ester intermediates. 3. Its precise biochemical role remained unresolved in this review.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Induction pattern differs from CER6, indicating distinct developmental/environmental regulation.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Protein of unknown precise biochemical function
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: cer3 mutants show strong reduction of aldehydes, alkanes, secondary alcohols, and ketones, with elevated C30 primary alcohols
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CER3 is a key determinant of wax production via the alkane-forming pathway. 2. Mutants are depleted in aldehydes, alkanes, secondary alcohols, and ketones. 3. CER3 expression is proposed to be under CER7-mediated post-transcriptional regulatory control.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Precise biochemical function was not established in this review.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Ectopic GPAT5 expression alters stem cuticular wax composition by increasing free VLCFAs and monoacylglycerols at the expense of standard wax components
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPAT5 is involved primarily in aliphatic suberin and root wax-related metabolism. 2. Ectopic expression in stems diverts VLCFA substrates away from standard wax products. 3. This provides evidence for shared precursor pools and competition between suberin-like and wax pathways.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review highlights it as a common step competing for wax acyl-CoA substrates, not as a canonical wax-pathway enzyme in stems.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Precursor supply from plastid fatty acid synthesis to wax pathway
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: free fatty acids released from acyl-ACP
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes fatty acyl-ACP thioesters to release free fatty acids for export from plastid toward wax biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of FATB reduces total wax load in stems by about 50% and in leaves by about 20%, indicating reduced precursor flux into wax biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key precursor-partitioning control point, not formally established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: epidermal cuticle of stems and leaves
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FATB releases plastid acyl chains for export toward wax synthesis. 2. fatb mutants show substantial reductions in stem and leaf wax loads. 3. FATB likely controls partitioning of C16/C18 fatty acids between plastid membrane lipid synthesis and cuticular wax production.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review paper; included because mutant phenotype explicitly links FATB to wax precursor supply. The text notes that the exact proportion of C18 flux is unknown.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: fatty acyl-CoAs longer than C22 plus malonyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes condensation during elongation of fatty acyl-CoAs to generate VLCFA wax precursors
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CER6 is required for stem wax accumulation; cer6 mutants retain only about 10% of wild-type stem wax and show reduced levels of all wax components with accumulation of C24/C26 acyl groups
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: major wax-specific elongation control point in stems
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant analysis; transgenic sense suppression; localization; expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Transcriptionally regulated during development; induced by light and osmotic stress
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CER6 is the only wax-specific KCS characterized in Arabidopsis. 2. It functions in elongation of acyl-CoAs longer than C22 during VLCFA formation. 3. Strong reduction of CER6 almost abolishes stem wax accumulation, indicating little functional overlap in stems.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review notes seven other KCS genes are epidermis-upregulated, but their wax functions remain unclear; CER6 is the clearly established core elongase component.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: trans-2-enoyl-CoA intermediates of fatty acid elongation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces enoyl-CoA intermediates during each cycle of very-long-chain fatty acid elongation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: cer10 mutants show reduced levels of all wax components and retain about 40% of wild-type stem wax, indicating CER10/ECR is required for VLCFA-containing lipid synthesis including wax
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: required elongase component, not established as sole rate-limiting step
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CER10 is the Arabidopsis enoyl-CoA reductase of the elongase complex. 2. It is needed for synthesis of VLCFA-containing lipids including cuticular waxes, seed TAGs, and sphingolipids. 3. Residual VLCFA production in cer10 suggests either another ECR or compensating enzyme(s).
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes functional complementation in yeast and residual wax in cer10 mutants suggest redundancy or alternative activities.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Directly reduces very-long-chain acyl-CoAs through aldehyde-equivalent intermediate to primary alcohols
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CER4 is the major alcohol-forming FAR in Arabidopsis stems; cer4 mutants show reduced primary alcohols and wax esters
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: major branch enzyme for alcohol formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant characterization; genomic complementation; heterologous expression in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CER4 encodes the major FAR responsible for primary alcohol formation in Arabidopsis stems. 2. The enzyme can catalyze both reduction steps from acyl precursor to alcohol. 3. Alcohols produced by CER4 supply wax ester synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: When expressed in yeast, CER4 forms mainly C24 and C26 primary alcohols.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: primary alcohols and mostly C16 acyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Esterifies primary alcohols with acyl-CoA substrates to produce wax esters
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: WSD1 is responsible for cuticular wax ester formation in Arabidopsis stems; mutants have reduced wax esters
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: terminal branch enzyme, not established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant phenotype reported in review table; biochemical characterization referenced as unpublished observations
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. WSD1 encodes a WS/DGAT responsible for wax ester formation in Arabidopsis stems. 2. It preferentially uses C16 acyl-CoA together with wax alcohols. 3. This implies early acyl intermediates converge with later alcohol products for ester biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Evidence cited in the review includes unpublished observations.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: alkanes; likely secondary alcohol intermediates for second oxidation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates alkanes at a midchain position to form secondary alcohols and likely catalyzes a second oxidation to ketones
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: mah1 mutants show reduced secondary alcohols and ketones in stem wax
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: terminal oxidation step for this branch
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MAH1/CYP96A15 catalyzes conversion of alkanes to secondary alcohols and ketones. 2. It likely performs both oxidation steps in Arabidopsis stems. 3. Discovery of MAH1 validated the long-proposed alkane oxidation branch.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review considers MAH1 one of the best-characterized late wax pathway enzymes.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: wax biosynthesis via epidermis-specific control of alkane pathway gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: post-transcriptional mRNA degradation that relieves repression of a wax biosynthetic gene
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: mRNA encoding a proposed repressor of CER3 transcription; CER3 indirectly
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes CER3 expression indirectly by degrading the mRNA of a putative CER3 transcriptional repressor
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: cer7 mutants have strongly reduced stem wax loads and reduced levels of all stem wax components
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: condition- and tissue-specific positive regulator required for normal wax accumulation through maintenance of CER3 expression
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant analysis; mechanistic genetic inference described in review
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: epidermal cells at onset of wax biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CER7 encodes an exosome ribonuclease subunit with a unique role in epidermal wax regulation. 2. It degrades the mRNA of a proposed repressor, thereby allowing CER3 expression. 3. Loss of CER7 reduces stem wax accumulation to about 30% of wild type.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The direct mRNA target was unknown in this review; CER7 acts in post-transcriptional control rather than as a canonical TF.

Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: CER1; CER2; KCS1; LACS2; other lipid biosynthetic and trafficking genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Overexpression induces several wax-related genes and rapidly activates cutin biosynthetic genes; direct binding shown for LACS2 promoter
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression dramatically enhances wax accumulation and especially increases leaf alkane levels, producing glossy leaves
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: strong positive regulator of cuticle lipid accumulation, though wax control may be partly indirect
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: overexpression; RNA blot; microarray; promoter binding evidence for LACS2
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. WIN1/SHN1 overexpression strongly increases cuticular wax deposition. 2. It induces wax-related genes including CER1, CER2, and KCS1 and elevates leaf alkane levels. 3. Its effects on wax may be partly indirect because cutin genes are activated earlier than wax genes.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes that WIN1/SHN1 may regulate wax formation indirectly through cutin changes or downstream regulators.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Positive regulation inferred from overexpression phenotype
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression causes extreme leaf glossiness and increased wax deposition on leaves and stems
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SHN2 overexpression increases wax deposition. 2. Transgenics display glossy leaves and enhanced cuticle lipid accumulation. 3. SHN2 belongs to the WIN/SHINE regulatory family controlling epidermal lipid traits.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mechanistic direct targets were not specified in the review.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Positive regulation inferred from overexpression phenotype
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression causes extreme leaf glossiness and increased wax deposition on leaves and stems
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SHN3 overexpression enhances wax deposition on leaves and stems. 2. Transgenic plants show strong glossy phenotypes. 3. SHN3 is a member of the SHINE family affecting epidermal cuticle formation.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific downstream wax genes were not detailed for SHN3 in this review.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes wax accumulation in overexpression studies summarized in the review
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression dramatically enhances wax accumulation and causes glossy leaf phenotype in transgenic plants
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. WXP1 is a Medicago AP2/EREBP regulator that enhances wax production. 2. Overexpression produces a glossy phenotype. 3. It is one of the few transcription factors known at the time to affect wax biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review does not detail direct targets or accession number.
